claims_verified.claim: The flock-guard rework (_acquire_lock, queue.py:133-167) eliminates the double-acquisition race: concurrent reclaimers can no longer both win the same stale lock.
claims_verified.evidence: Real multiprocessing repro: 20 trials, each spawning two genuine OS processes racing run_lock() against the same pre-seeded stale lock (dead pid), synchronized to start via a barrier. Every trial yielded exactly one 'acquired' and one 'locked' (QueueLocked) outcome, never both-acquired. python3 -m pytest -q tests/test_queue.py -> 15 passed.

test_assessment: Go/nogo scope only: the previously-flagged invariant violation (concurrent stale-lock reclaim yielding two live holders) is resolved by serializing the whole check-and-write decision under a per-task flock guard (a lock type that is atomically scoped to the inode across processes and auto-released by the kernel on close/crash, so it cannot itself go stale). Verified both by code reading and by an independent real-process concurrency repro outside the existing test suite.
residual_risks: PID reuse: a recycled dead pid held by an unrelated process would still read as a live holder and refuse reclaim -- unchanged from before, not blocking.
